Project Python Foundations: FoodHub Data Analysis¶

Context¶

The number of restaurants in New York is increasing day by day. Lots of students and busy professionals rely on those restaurants due to their hectic lifestyles. Online food delivery service is a great option for them. It provides them with good food from their favorite restaurants. A food aggregator company FoodHub offers access to multiple restaurants through a single smartphone app.

The app allows the restaurants to receive a direct online order from a customer. The app assigns a delivery person from the company to pick up the order after it is confirmed by the restaurant. The delivery person then uses the map to reach the restaurant and waits for the food package. Once the food package is handed over to the delivery person, he/she confirms the pick-up in the app and travels to the customer's location to deliver the food. The delivery person confirms the drop-off in the app after delivering the food package to the customer. The customer can rate the order in the app. The food aggregator earns money by collecting a fixed margin of the delivery order from the restaurants.

Objective¶

The food aggregator company has stored the data of the different orders made by the registered customers in their online portal. They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand of different restaurants which will help them in enhancing their customer experience. Suppose you are hired as a Data Scientist in this company and the Data Science team has shared some of the key questions that need to be answered. Perform the data analysis to find answers to these questions that will help the company to improve the business.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different data related to a food order. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

Data Dictionary¶

  • order_id: Unique ID of the order
  • customer_id: ID of the customer who ordered the food
  • restaurant_name: Name of the restaurant
  • cuisine_type: Cuisine ordered by the customer
  • cost_of_the_order: Cost of the order
  • day_of_the_week: Indicates whether the order is placed on a weekday or weekend (The weekday is from Monday to Friday and the weekend is Saturday and Sunday)
  • rating: Rating given by the customer out of 5
  • food_preparation_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the restaurant to prepare the food. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the restaurant's order confirmation and the delivery person's pick-up confirmation.
  • delivery_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the delivery person to deliver the food package. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the delivery person's pick-up confirmation and drop-off information

import seaborn as sns

Question 4: Check the statistical summary of the data. What is the minimum, average, and maximum time it takes for food to be prepared once an order is placed? [2 marks]¶

In [31]:
# Write your code here
min_time = data['food_preparation_time'].min()
ave_time = data['food_preparation_time'].mean()
max_time = data['food_preparation_time'].max()
print('The minimum time it takes for food to be prepared once an order is placed is', min_time)
print('The average time it takes for food to be prepared once an order is placed is', ave_time)
print('The maximum time it takes for food to be prepared once an order is placed is', max_time)

The minimum time it takes for food to be prepared once an order is placed is 20
The average time it takes for food to be prepared once an order is placed is 27.371970495258168
The maximum time it takes for food to be prepared once an order is placed is 35

Observations:¶

The minimum time for food preparation is 20 minutes, the average is approximately 27.37 minutes, and the maximum is 35 minutes.

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

Univariate Analysis¶

Question 6: Explore all the variables and provide observations on their distributions. (Generally, histograms, boxplots, countplots, etc. are used for univariate exploration.) [9 marks]¶

In [33]:
# Set a clean style
sns.set(style="whitegrid")

# 1. Numerical variables
num_cols = ["cost_of_the_order", "rating", "food_preparation_time", "delivery_time"]

for col in num_cols:
    plt.figure(figsize=(12,5))

    # Histogram
    plt.subplot(1,2,1)
    sns.histplot(data[col], kde=True, bins=20)
    plt.title(f"Distribution of {col}")

    # Boxplot
    plt.subplot(1,2,2)
    sns.boxplot(x=data[col])
    plt.title(f"Boxplot of {col}")

    plt.show()

# 2. Categorical variables
cat_cols = ["cuisine_type", "day_of_the_week"]

for col in cat_cols:
    plt.figure(figsize=(12,5))
    sns.countplot(y=data[col], order=data[col].value_counts().index)
    plt.title(f"Countplot of {col}")
    plt.show()

Observations:

Numerical fields like cost_of_the_order and food_preparation_time are close to being normally distributed.

The delivery_time is slightly right-skewed.

Categorical fields like cuisine_type show that American cuisine occurs the most often.

Orders occur more on weekends than weekdays.

There are a number of "Not given" values in the rating column this greatly affect the analysis on customer rating.

Multivariate Analysis¶

Question 12: Perform a multivariate analysis to explore relationships between the important variables in the dataset. (It is a good idea to explore relations between numerical variables as well as relations between numerical and categorical variables) [10 marks]¶

In [ ]:
# Numerical vs Numerical
# Correlation heatmap for numerical variables

plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.heatmap(data.corr(numeric_only=True), annot=True, cmap="coolwarm", fmt=".2f")
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap (Numerical Variables)")
plt.show()

# Scatterplot for two important numerical features cost_of_the_order &
# food_preparation_time
if 'cost_of_the_order' in data.columns and 'food_preparation_time' in data.columns:
    plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
    sns.scatterplot(x="food_preparation_time", y="cost_of_the_order", data=data, alpha=0.6)
    plt.title("Cost of Order vs Food Preparation Time")
    plt.show()

# Scartterplot of Delivery Time vs Cost of the order
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.scatterplot(x='cost_of_the_order', y='delivery_time', data=data, hue='day_of_the_week', alpha=0.7)
plt.title('Total Delivery Time vs Cost')
plt.xlabel('Cost of the Order')
plt.ylabel('Total Delivery Time (min)')
plt.show()

# Delivery Time vs Cost of the order
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.boxplot(x="delivery_time", y="cost_of_the_order", data=data)
plt.title("Cost of Order vs Delivery Time")
plt.show()

# Delivery Time vs Food Preparation Time
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.boxplot(x="food_preparation_time", y="delivery_time", data=data)
plt.title("Delivery Time vs Food Preparation Time")
plt.show()

# Pairplot to see relationships among key numerical variables
num_cols = data.select_dtypes(include=['int64','float64']).columns
sns.pairplot(data[num_cols], diag_kind="kde")
plt.show()

#. Numerical vs Categorical Analysis

# Boxplot of order cost across different restaurants
top_10_restaurants = data['restaurant_name'].value_counts().head(10).index
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
sns.boxplot(x="restaurant_name", y="cost_of_the_order",data=data[data['restaurant_name'].isin(top_10_restaurants)])
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Cost of Orders across Top 10 Restaurants")
plt.show()

# Line plot
plt.figure(figsize=(15,7))
sns.lineplot(data=data, x="cuisine_type", y="cost_of_the_order", hue='delivery_time', estimator='sum', ci=False)
plt.title('Cost of Order by Cuisine Type')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.show()


# Delivery time vs Categorical variable (cuisine_type)
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.boxplot(x="cuisine_type", y="delivery_time", data=data)
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Delivery Time across Cuisine Types")
plt.show()

# Day of the week vs Delivery Time
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.boxplot(x="day_of_the_week", y="delivery_time", data=data)
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title("Delivery Time across Day of the Week")

# Boxplot of delivery time vs day_of_the_week
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.boxplot(x="day_of_the_week", y="delivery_time", data=data)
plt.title("Delivery Time by Day of the Week")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

# Boxplotof cost of order vs cuisine type
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
sns.boxplot(x="cuisine_type", y="cost_of_the_order", data=data)
plt.title("Cost of Orders by Cuisine Type")
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.show()

# Boxplotof Food Preparation Time vs Cuisine type
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 7))
sns.boxplot(x='cuisine_type', y='food_preparation_time', data=data)
plt.title('Food Preparation Time by Cuisine Type')
plt.xlabel('Cuisine Type')
plt.ylabel('Preparation Time (min)')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

/tmp/ipython-input-2038333438.py:54: FutureWarning: 

The `ci` parameter is deprecated. Use `errorbar=('ci', False)` for the same effect.

  sns.lineplot(data=data, x="cuisine_type", y="cost_of_the_order", hue='delivery_time', estimator='sum', ci=False)

Observation:

  1. There is no clear trend between food preparation time and cost of the order.
  2. The cost of an order doesn't seem to depend heavily on how long it takes to prepare the food.
  3. Weekend orders seem to have higher delivery times compared to weekday orders possibly indicating busier conditions
  4. There's no clear correlation between the cost of the order and total delivery time.
  5. Delivery times are consistent across different food preparation times, with medians clustering around 27.5-28 minutes.
  6. Restaurants like "Blue Moon Sushi" show less variability in order costs compared to others.

Question 13: The company wants to provide a promotional offer in the advertisement of the restaurants. The condition to get the offer is that the restaurants must have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. Find the restaurants fulfilling the criteria to get the promotional offer. [3 marks]¶

In [ ]:
# Write the code here
data['rating_convert'] = pd.to_numeric(data['rating'], errors='coerce')
# We need to group by restaurant to calculate rating count and average rating
restaurant_stats = data.groupby('restaurant_name').agg(
    rating_count=('rating_convert', 'count'),
    avg_rating=('rating_convert', 'mean')
).reset_index()
restaurant_stats.columns = ['restaurant_name', 'rating_count', 'average_rating']
# Filter restaurants based on the conditions
promotional_restaurants = restaurant_stats[(restaurant_stats['rating_count'] > 50) & (restaurant_stats['average_rating'] > 4)]

# Print the result
print(promotional_restaurants)

               restaurant_name  rating_count  average_rating
20   Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken            64        4.328125
21           Blue Ribbon Sushi            73        4.219178
136                Shake Shack           133        4.278195
153          The Meatball Shop            84        4.511905

Observations:¶

Four of the restaurants are eligible for the promotion.

• Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken, 64 reviews, average review 4.33 • Blue Ribbon Sushi, 73 reviews, average rating 4.22 • Shake Shack, 133 reviews, 4.28 average rating • The Meatball Shop, 84 reviews, average rating 4.51

Question 14: The company charges the restaurant 25% on the orders having cost greater than 20 dollars and 15% on the orders having cost greater than 5 dollars. Find the net revenue generated by the company across all orders. [3 marks]¶

In [ ]:
# Write the code here
data['commission'] = data['cost_of_the_order'].apply(
    lambda x: x * 0.25 if x > 20 else (x * 0.15 if x > 5 else 0)
)

# Calculate net revenue
net_revenue = data['commission'].sum()

print(f"Net revenue generated by the company across all orders: {net_revenue:.2f}")

Net revenue generated by the company across all orders: 6166.30

Observations:¶

The net revenue after applying 25% on orders costing more than 20 dollars and 15% on the orders having cost greater than 5 dollars is 6166.30

Question 15: The company wants to analyze the total time required to deliver the food. What percentage of orders take more than 60 minutes to get delivered from the time the order is placed? (The food has to be prepared and then delivered.) [2 marks]¶

In [ ]:
# Write the code here
data["total_time"] = data["food_preparation_time"] + data["delivery_time"]
orders_over_60 = (data["total_time"] > 60).sum()
total_orders = len(data)
percentage_over_60 = (orders_over_60 / total_orders) * 100
print(f"Percentage of orders taking more than 60 mins: {percentage_over_60:.2f}%")

Percentage of orders taking more than 60 mins: 10.54%

Observations:¶

Based on the analysis 10.54% of the orders have a total delivery time exceeding 60 minutes, encompassing both food preparation and delivery phases.

Question 16: The company wants to analyze the delivery time of the orders on weekdays and weekends. How does the mean delivery time vary during weekdays and weekends? [2 marks]¶

In [ ]:
# Write the code here
mean_delivery = data.groupby("day_of_the_week")["delivery_time"].mean().reset_index()
print(mean_delivery)

  day_of_the_week  delivery_time
0         Weekday      28.340037
1         Weekend      22.470022

Observations:¶

Weekday average delivery time is 28.34 minutes. Weekend average delivery time is 22.47 minutes. This means Weekend deliveries take longer.

Conclusion and Recommendations¶

Question 17: What are your conclusions from the analysis? What recommendations would you like to share to help improve the business? (You can use cuisine type and feedback ratings to drive your business recommendations.) [6 marks]¶

Conclusions:¶

  1. American food receives the highest proportion of orders. Italian and Japanese foods are also in high demand. French, Vietnamese, and Southern foods receive the fewest orders.

  2. More than one-third of all of the orders, 736, have no customer rating. That is a large feedback gap. Of the ones that are rated, the vast majority are positive. There are 588 five star and 386 four star ratings in the data. Just 188 orders were rated with three stars.

  3. The order to delivery average time is 51.5 minutes. Preparation of food takes on average 27.4 minutes. Delivery takes on average 24.2 minutes. Weekends see more orders than weekdays.

4.There is no strong correlation between preparation time, delivery time, and order cost.

Recommendations:¶

  1. Create a system of gathering more ratings from the customers. This will provide more consolidated data. Provide a small incentive to the customers for rating orders.

  2. Focus more on popular cuisine like American and Japanese to boost revenue throughing higher sales.For less popular cuisine such as Vietnamese or French, determine the reason behind the low orders. Run targeted campaigns to take them to the top.

  3. Remove the long waiting time for certain orders. Food preparation processes for restaurants that take more than 30 minutes to prepare must be optimized. It will enhance the overall customer experience.

  4. Take advantage of positive reviews by featuring highly rated restaurants in your app. Show great customers' reviews to build trust.

  5. Use promotions to enhance weekday orders. This will spread demand evenly across the week.
